commonProblems.h2: Common Hyundai Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Hyundai's reputation for affordability means many owners keep their cars well beyond warranty, but certain engine families dominate replacement demand: the Theta II GDi petrol units (2.0/2.4L) and CRDi diesel engines (1.6/2.0L). Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. The problem groups below break down real UK repair costs versus replacement, so you can decide whether a fix or a fresh engine gives you the best value for your i30, Tucson, or Santa Fe.

problems.h4: Theta II GDi Bearing Failure – Why 2.0/2.4L Petrol Engines Seize Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai Tucson, Santa Fe, i40, Sonata (2011–2019, G4NA/G4NC/G4KJ 2.0L & 2.4L GDi)
problems.rootCause: Inadequate oil flow to the connecting rod bearings combined with tight manufacturing tolerances causes accelerated wear, especially under short-trip driving or delayed oil changes.
problems.recommendation: On a Tucson or Santa Fe worth £4,000–£8,000, we rarely recommend bearing-only repairs once knocking is audible — the risk of hidden crank damage is too high. A rebuilt Theta II with upgraded components from our network delivers peace of mind and often costs less than a dealer-led partial repair.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Theta II engine prices for your Tucson or Santa Fe

problems.h4: Gamma GDi Timing Chain Stretch – Why 1.0–1.6L Petrol Engines Rattle and Fail
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i10, i20, i30, Tucson (2011–2018, G3LA/G3LC/G4FG 1.0–1.6L GDi)
problems.rootCause: Weak hydraulic tensioners and plastic guide wear cause the chain to stretch; oil starvation from infrequent changes accelerates the process, leading to timing drift.
problems.recommendation: If your Gamma engine is rattling cold but still runs smoothly, a preventative chain kit from a specialist offers excellent value. Once the chain has jumped and caused valve contact, however, a rebuilt engine is almost always cheaper and more reliable than a head-only repair.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt Gamma engine replacements for your i30 or Tucson

problems.h4: CRDi Diesel EGR/DPF Clogging – Why Oil Dilution Kills 1.6/2.0L Diesels
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i30, ix35, Tucson, Santa Fe (2010–2020, D4FB/D4HA 1.6/2.0L CRDi)
problems.rootCause: Short-trip driving prevents DPF regeneration; soot builds in the EGR cooler and mixes with oil, causing dilution that thins lubrication and accelerates bearing wear.
problems.recommendation: For CRDi engines showing early warning signs (increased oil level, DPF light), an EGR blank and DPF clean can buy time. But if the dipstick shows fuel-thinned oil or you hear bearing knock, a rebuilt CRDi from our network is the only route that guarantees reliability and warranty protection.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt CRDi engine prices for your i30 or Santa Fe

problems.h4: Kappa T-GDi Turbo Failure – Why Small Petrol Turbos Burn Out Early
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i10, i20, Bayon (2017–2023, G3LA/G3LB 1.0L T-GDi)
problems.rootCause: High exhaust temperatures in the compact turbo housing cause oil coking in feed lines; carbon buildup on the wastegate leads to sticking and over-boost events.
problems.recommendation: If your Kappa engine is just throwing boost codes with no smoke or noise, a specialist actuator repair can be cost-effective. But if you see blue smoke or hear turbo whine, debris has likely entered the cylinders — at that point, a rebuilt engine with an upgraded turbo is the only route that protects your investment.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt Kappa engine replacements for your i10 or i20

problems.h4: Nu MPi Oil Consumption – Why 1.8/2.0L Petrol Engines Burn Oil Excessively
problems.affectedModels: Hyundai i30, i40, Sonata (2012–2019, G4ND/G4NJ 1.8/2.0L MPi)
problems.rootCause: Piston ring land design and valve stem seal hardening allow oil to pass into the combustion chamber; exacerbated by infrequent oil changes and short trips.
problems.recommendation: For Nu engines consuming less than 1L per 1,000 miles, a PCV upgrade and high-mileage oil can extend life affordably. Once consumption exceeds that threshold or you see blue exhaust smoke, a rebuilt Nu engine is almost always cheaper and more reliable than an in-situ ring job.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt Nu engine prices for your i40 or Sonata

engineTypes.h2: Hyundai Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Hyundai engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle's age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Hyundai Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price — ideal for i30, Tucson and Santa Fe owners addressing common CRDi or Theta II wear issues.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,200 – £3,500 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Hyundai Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points — such as uprated bearing shells for Theta II petrol units and refurbished turbos for CRDi diesels — for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Hyundai Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available — perfect for i10, i20 and Getz owners seeking a cheap, quick solution.

types.title: Hyundai Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine with maximum peace of mind.

types.title: Hyundai Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn't had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models like Lantra, Accent or early Getz where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Hyundai Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Hyundai specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty — the simplest way to get back on the road without arranging separate garage labour.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £400 – £900 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Hyundai engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Hyundai model today.

engineSizes.h2: Hyundai Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Hyundai engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Hyundai 1.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.4‑litre CRDi diesel was Hyundai's entry‑level oil‑burner for small hatchbacks, most commonly fitted to the i20, i30 and ix20 from the early‑2010s. Built around the D4FC engine family, these units are generally robust but can suffer from EGR and DPF‑related issues when used predominantly on short trips — a failure pattern that often leads owners to seek replacement rather than repeated repairs. Engine code(s): D4FC Compatible models (UK): i20, i30, ix20 (2010–2017) Production years: 2010–2017 Common failure points: EGR valve clogging, DPF regeneration failures leading to oil dilution
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 1.4L diesel engines

items.title: Hyundai 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6‑litre CRDi is Hyundai's most widely fitted diesel displacement across the UK range, powering the i30, Tucson, i40 and ix35 from 2010 onward . Primarily using the D4FB and D4FE engine codes, this size offers a strong balance of economy and torque, but is prone to EGR cooler leaks and DPF‑induced oil dilution — two issues that frequently escalate into bearing wear and drive replacement demand . Engine code(s): D4FB, D4FE, D4FD Compatible models (UK): i30, Tucson, i40, ix35, Santa Fe (2010–present) Production years: 2010–present Common failure points: EGR cooler failure, DPF clogging causing oil dilution and accelerated bearing wear
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Hyundai 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 2.0‑litre CRDi diesel, built around the D4HA and D4EB families, was the workhorse for larger models like the Tucson, Santa Fe and i40 throughout the 2010s . These engines deliver strong towing capability but are susceptible to turbocharger wear, high‑pressure injector failure, and EGR‑system carbon buildup — failure modes that often make a rebuilt replacement more cost‑effective than extensive in‑situ repairs. Engine code(s): D4HA, D4EB, D4EA Compatible models (UK): Tucson, Santa Fe, i40, ix35, Sonata (2006–2020) Production years: 2006–2020 Common failure points: Turbocharger bearing failure, injector wear, EGR carbon buildup
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Hyundai 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2‑litre CRDi, sharing architecture with the 2.0L but with increased displacement for the Santa Fe and Grand Santa Fe, was Hyundai's largest diesel offering for the UK market . Using the D4HA and D4EB codes, these engines face similar failure patterns to the 2.0L but under higher thermal loads — making turbo failure, injector issues, and timing component wear the most common drivers for replacement. Engine code(s): D4HA, D4EB Compatible models (UK): Santa Fe, Grand Santa Fe (2006–2020) Production years: 2006–2020 Common failure points: Turbo failure under load, high‑pressure injector wear, timing chain tensioner wear
items.cta: Get quotes for Hyundai 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Hyundai 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.0‑litre petrol units, part of the Kappa family (G3LA/G3LB), power the i10, i20 and Bayon with both naturally‑aspirated and turbocharged (T‑GDi) variants . While the NA versions are generally reliable, the T‑GDi variants can suffer from turbocharger oil‑coking and carbon buildup on intake valves — issues that, if ignored, may lead to costly replacement rather than repair . Engine code(s): G3LA, G3LB Compatible models (UK): i10, i20, Bayon (2017–present) Production years: 2017–present Common failure points: Turbo oil‑coking (T‑GDi), carbon buildup on intake valves (GDi)
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2‑litre Kappa G4LA is Hyundai's small‑capacity naturally‑aspirated petrol engine, fitted to the i10 and i20 from the late‑2000s through the 2010s . Known for simplicity and low running costs, these units rarely suffer catastrophic failures, but occasional coil‑pack or sensor issues can prompt owners to consider a low‑mileage used replacement for maximum value. Engine code(s): G4LA Compatible models (UK): i10, i20 (2008–2020) Production years: 2008–2020 Common failure points: Ignition coil failure, occasional sensor faults (generally reliable)
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.4‑litre petrol displacement spans both the Gamma (G4FA/G4FC) and Kappa (G4LD) families, appearing in the i20, i30 and ix20 from the late‑2000s . Early Gamma GDi variants can experience timing‑chain stretch and carbon buildup, while later MPi versions are more robust — but any 1.4L showing excessive oil consumption or rattling may warrant a rebuilt replacement . Engine code(s): G4FA, G4FC, G4LD Compatible models (UK): i20, i30, ix20 (2008–2017) Production years: 2008–2017 Common failure points: Timing‑chain stretch (early Gamma GDi), carbon buildup on intake valves
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6‑litre Gamma G4FG and Nu G4FD engines power the i30, Tucson and i40 across the 2010s, offering a balance of performance and efficiency for UK drivers . While generally dependable, Gamma GDi variants can develop timing‑chain noise and carbon deposits, and any 1.6L showing knocking or high oil consumption should be assessed for bearing wear before committing to minor repairs. Engine code(s): G4FG, G4FD Compatible models (UK): i30, Tucson, i40, Elantra (2010–present) Production years: 2010–present Common failure points: Timing‑chain stretch (Gamma GDi), carbon buildup, occasional oil consumption (Nu)
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.8‑litre Nu G4NB engine was fitted to the i30 and i40 during the early‑to‑mid 2010s as a mid‑range petrol option . These units can develop excessive oil consumption due to piston‑ring and valve‑seal wear, a failure pattern that often makes a reconditioned replacement more economical than an in‑situ ring job on higher‑mileage cars . Engine code(s): G4NB Compatible models (UK): i30, i40 (2011–2017) Production years: 2011–2017 Common failure points: Excessive oil consumption from piston‑ring and valve‑seal wear
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0‑litre Theta II (G4NA/G4NC/G4KJ) and Nu (G4ND) families dominate Hyundai's mid‑size petrol range, powering the Tucson, Santa Fe, i40 and Sonata from the late‑2000s . Theta II units are notorious for connecting‑rod bearing failure due to oil‑flow limitations — a catastrophic issue that almost always requires a full rebuilt replacement with upgraded internals rather than a partial repair . Engine code(s): G4NA, G4NC, G4KJ, G4ND Compatible models (UK): Tucson, Santa Fe, i40, Sonata (2009–2019) Production years: 2009–2019 Common failure points: Theta II – connecting‑rod bearing failure; Nu – occasional oil consumption
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 2.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 2.4‑litre Theta II G4KC engine was reserved for larger models like the Santa Fe and Sonata, offering extra torque for towing and load‑carrying . Sharing the same bearing‑design vulnerability as the 2.0L Theta II, these engines are prone to the same catastrophic bearing failure under oil‑starvation conditions — making a rebuilt unit with upgraded components the only reliable long‑term solution . Engine code(s): G4KC Compatible models (UK): Santa Fe, Sonata (2009–2018) Production years: 2009–2018 Common failure points: Connecting‑rod bearing failure (same root cause as 2.0L Theta II)
items.cta: Compare Hyundai 2.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai 1.6L Hybrid Engines
items.description: Hyundai's 1.6‑litre hybrid system, combining a Kappa‑family petrol engine with an electric motor and battery pack, appears in the Ioniq, Tucson Hybrid and Kona Hybrid from 2016 onward . While the petrol component shares failure patterns with conventional 1.6L units, hybrid‑specific concerns centre on battery health and inverter reliability — making specialist assessment essential before pursuing replacement. System / motor code(s): G4LE + electric motor (TMED system) Compatible models (UK): Ioniq Hybrid, Tucson Hybrid, Kona Hybrid (2016–present) Production years: 2016–present Important notes: Battery health inspection critical; electric motor failure rare but specialist‑only repair
items.cta: Enquire about Hyundai 1.6L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Hyundai Electric Motors
items.description: Hyundai's fully electric models — including the Kona Electric, Ioniq Electric and Ioniq 5/6 — use permanent‑magnet synchronous motors rather than traditional displacement engines . Motor failure is exceptionally rare, but high‑voltage battery degradation or inverter faults can necessitate specialist replacement; these systems require certified high‑voltage technicians and are not suitable for general engine‑replacement marketplaces. System / motor code(s): PE series (varies by model/year) Compatible models (UK): Kona Electric, Ioniq Electric, Ioniq 5, Ioniq 6 (2018–present) Production years: 2018–present Important notes: High‑voltage safety certification required; battery health assessment essential before any replacement decision Closing line: Not sure which engine size your Hyundai needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Hyundai specialists. items.title: Hyundai Diesel Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Hyundai's CRDi diesel range powers the i30, Tucson and Santa Fe across two decades, with the 1.6L and 2.0L units accounting for the majority of UK replacement enquiries due to EGR/DPF‑related wear. Common Hyundai Diesel Engine Families: D4FB – 1.6L CRDi workhorse (i30, Tucson, i40), prone to EGR cooler leaks and DPF oil dilution D4HA – 2.0L/2.2L CRDi (Santa Fe, Tucson), robust but susceptible to turbo bearing wear under load D4FC – 1.4L CRDi (i20, ix20), entry‑level diesel with simpler emissions hardware, fewer catastrophic failures D4EB – earlier 2.0L CRDi (ix35, Sonata), known for high‑pressure injector wear on high‑mileage examples Found In: i30 (FD, GD, PD generations; 2007–present) – 1.6L CRDi dominant Tucson (LM, TL, NX4; 2010–present) – 1.6L & 2.0L CRDi across all generations Santa Fe (CM, DM, TM; 2006–2020) – 2.0L & 2.2L CRDi for towing capability i40 (VF; 2011–2019) – 1.6L & 2.0L CRDi fleet favourite ix35 (EL; 2010–2015) – transitional model with 1.6L/2.0L CRDi Known For (Brand‑wide): Strong motorway fuel economy across 1.6L–2.2L CRDi range, ideal for high‑mileage UK drivers EGR cooler and DPF clogging on short‑journey cars leads to oil dilution and accelerated bearing wear ~65% of Hyundai engine replacement enquiries in the UK are for CRDi diesel units Timing chain tensioner wear on pre‑2015 D4HA units can cause rattling before catastrophic failure Typical Diesel Models (UK): i30 1.6 CRDi SE Tucson 2.0 CRDi Premium Santa Fe 2.2 CRDi Ultimate i40 1.6 CRDi Business ix35 2.0 CRDi Style

items.title: Hyundai Petrol Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Hyundai's petrol lineup spans economical Kappa units in the i10 to powerful Theta II engines in the Santa Fe, with GDi technology dominating post‑2010 models and replacement demand driven by bearing and timing‑chain issues. Common Hyundai Petrol Engine Families: G4NA/G4NC – Theta II 2.0L/2.4L GDi (Tucson, Santa Fe), notorious for connecting‑rod bearing failure under oil starvation G4FG – Gamma 1.6L GDi (i30, Tucson), generally reliable but timing‑chain stretch reported on early units G3LA/G3LB – Kappa 1.0L T‑GDi (i10, i20, Bayon), turbo oil‑coking risk on short‑trip driving G4LD – Kappa 1.4L T‑GDi (i30, Veloster), performance‑oriented with carbon buildup on intake valves Found In: i10 (PA, IA, BC3; 2008–present) – 1.0L–1.2L Kappa NA and T‑GDi i20 (PB, GB, BC3; 2008–present) – 1.0L–1.4L Kappa/Gamma range i30 (FD, GD, PD; 2007–present) – 1.4L–2.0L Gamma/Nu/Theta II petrol options Tucson (LM, TL, NX4; 2010–present) – 1.6L Gamma T‑GDi and 2.0L Theta II Santa Fe (CM, DM, TM; 2006–2020) – 2.0L/2.4L Theta II for higher‑spec trims Known For (Brand‑wide): Theta II GDi engines (2009–2019) account for the highest‑value petrol replacement jobs due to bearing failure Gamma GDi units can develop timing‑chain noise after 80k miles if oil changes are delayed Kappa T‑GDi engines benefit from frequent oil changes to prevent turbo oil‑coking on urban‑use cars MPi (non‑GDi) variants like the Nu 1.8L are less prone to carbon buildup but can suffer oil consumption on high mileage Typical Petrol Models (UK): i10 1.0 MPI SE i20 1.0 T‑GDi N Line i30 1.4 T‑GDi Premium Tucson 2.0 GDi SE Santa Fe 2.4 GDi Ultimate
items.cta: Compare Hyundai Petrol engine prices

items.title: Hyundai Hybrid / Electrified Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Hyundai's TMED (Transmission Mounted Electric Device) hybrid system pairs Kappa or Gamma petrol engines with an electric motor and battery, appearing in the Ioniq, Tucson Hybrid and Kona Hybrid from 2016 onward. Common Hyundai Hybrid Engine Families: G4LE + TMED motor – 1.6L Kappa hybrid (Ioniq, Tucson Hybrid, Kona Hybrid), most common UK hybrid replacement enquiry How Hyundai Hybrid Systems Work (brand‑wide): Petrol engine and electric motor can drive wheels independently or together via a 6‑speed dual‑clutch transmission Regenerative braking recharges the 1.56kWh (HEV) or 13.8kWh (PHEV) battery without plug‑in for standard hybrids Battery health inspection is essential during any engine replacement quote to avoid mismatched system diagnostics Found In: Ioniq Hybrid (AE; 2016–2022) – 1.6L GDi + 32kW electric motor, HEV only Tucson Hybrid (NX4; 2021–present) – 1.6L T‑GDi + 44kW motor, HEV and PHEV variants Kona Hybrid (OS; 2021–present) – 1.6L GDi + 32kW motor, HEV only Important Notes (Brand‑wide): Hybrid engine replacement is rare but available from specialist suppliers who can bench‑test the petrol unit separately Always request a hybrid system health report alongside the engine quote to confirm battery and inverter status PHEV versions share the same petrol engine block as non‑hybrids, simplifying parts sourcing for the combustion side
items.cta: Enquire about Hyundai Hybrid engine availability

items.title: Hyundai Electric Powertrains
items.description: Short descriptor: Hyundai's fully electric models use permanent‑magnet synchronous motors rather than traditional engines; motor failure is exceptionally rare, but we connect you with UK high‑voltage specialists for battery or inverter repairs. Common Hyundai Electric Motors: PE150/PE170 – 150kW/170kW motor (Kona Electric 64kWh, Ioniq Electric), single‑motor FWD layout PE300 – dual‑motor AWD system (Ioniq 5/6), up to 239kW combined output How Hyundai Electric Powertrains Work: Single or dual permanent‑magnet motors draw power from a liquid‑cooled lithium‑ion battery pack (39–77.4kWh) WLTP ranges span ~190 miles (Ioniq Electric) to ~300 miles (Kona Electric 64kWh, Ioniq 5 LR) Motor failure is extremely rare; high‑voltage battery module repair or inverter replacement is more common in the UK replacement market Found In: Kona Electric (OS; 2018–present) – 39kWh or 64kWh battery options Ioniq Electric (AE; 2016–2022) – 28kWh or 38.3kWh battery Ioniq 5 (NE; 2021–present) – 58kWh or 77.4kWh, RWD or AWD Ioniq 6 (CE; 2022–present) – 53kWh or 77.4kWh, RWD or AWD Important Notes: No traditional "engine" to replace – we connect you with HV battery and motor repair specialists across the UK Labour rates for high‑voltage work differ from combustion engine repairs; always confirm specialist certification Battery health reports (SoH %) are critical when evaluating a used electric Hyundai for potential powertrain investment

engineYears.h2: Hyundai Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers Hyundai's complete UK engine timeline from 1998 to the present across every major model line - from the early Beta and Sirius diesel families through the Theta II controversy, the CRDi diesel era, the Smartstream generation, and the current hybrid and electric powertrain range - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year and compare rebuilt Hyundai engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: Beta Petrol & Early D4 Diesel Era
years.description: Short intro: The late 1990s Hyundai UK range was built around the iron-block Beta four-cylinder petrol family alongside early D4 diesel units, establishing the foundations of the model range that would carry through to the Accent, Coupe, and first-generation Sonata. Key Changes G4GC Beta 2.0 16v four-cylinder dominant across Coupe, Sonata, and Elantra G4EA Beta 1.6 introduced for entry-level Accent and Elantra applications D4EA 2.0 CRDi diesel introduced (2000–2001) - Hyundai's first common-rail diesel for Sonata and early Santa Fe Sirius D4BH 2.5 diesel continues in Terracan and H-1 commercial applications Euro 2/3 in force across the UK range Main Engines G4GC - 2.0 petrol inline-four Beta (Coupe, Elantra, Sonata, Tucson early) G4EA - 1.6 petrol inline-four Beta (Accent, Elantra) D4EA - 2.0 CRDi diesel (Sonata, Santa Fe first gen, Elantra CRDi) D4BH - 2.5 diesel (Terracan, H-1) Popular Models Hyundai Coupe Hyundai Elantra Hyundai Sonata Hyundai Santa Fe (first generation) Hyundai Accent Known For G4GC Beta 2.0 is one of Hyundai's most durable petrol engines - iron block, conventional chain-driven DOHC; regularly achieves 150,000+ miles with basic maintenance; widely used in motorsport rebuilds D4EA 2.0 CRDi marks Hyundai's credible entry into common-rail diesel - well-regarded for durability by UK used car specialists but injector O-ring seal leaks and EGR valve carbon deposits are the most commonly cited issues at high mileage Beta family engines can suffer oil consumption from valve stem seal wear beyond 90,000 miles - a known trait across the G4GC and G4EA units Early Hyundai UK models generate modest replacement engine demand - the real peak replacement volume arrives with the post-2006 CRDi fleet [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: G4GC, G4EA, G4EB, D4EA, D4BH
years.cta: Find rebuilt 1998–2002 Hyundai Beta and CRDi engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Tucson & Santa Fe Diesel Volume Begins, G4KA/G4KC Theta Intro
years.description: Short intro: The arrival of the first-generation Tucson (2004) and the second-generation Santa Fe brought Hyundai's 2.0 CRDi and 2.2 CRDi diesel engines to mass-market UK buyers, while the Theta four-cylinder petrol family was introduced to replace Beta in medium models. Key Changes Tucson (2004) launched with D4EA 2.0 CRDi diesel and G4GC petrol - Hyundai's first compact SUV in UK D4EB 2.2 CRDi introduced (2005–2006) - higher-displacement diesel for Santa Fe and Sonata Theta G4KC 2.4 and G4KA 2.0 introduced (2005) - aluminium block, new DOHC architecture replacing Beta in Sonata NF Santa Fe second generation (2006) - 2.2 D4EB diesel and 3.3 Lambda V6 petrol options Euro 4 enforcement begins Main Engines D4EA - 2.0 CRDi diesel (Tucson, Elantra, Sonata) D4EB - 2.2 CRDi diesel (Santa Fe second gen, Sonata) G4KC - 2.4 Theta petrol inline-four (Sonata NF) G4KA - 2.0 Theta petrol inline-four (Sonata, Tucson petrol) G4GC - 2.0 Beta petrol continued (Coupe, Elantra) G6EA - 3.3 Lambda V6 petrol (Santa Fe) Popular Models Hyundai Tucson (first generation) Hyundai Santa Fe (second generation) Hyundai Sonata (NF) Hyundai Coupe Hyundai Elantra Known For D4EA 2.0 CRDi in first-gen Tucson is the single highest-volume replacement engine search for Hyundai in the UK across this era - timing belt failure, EGR carbon fouling, and injector issues from 80,000 miles are the primary concerns for independent specialists D4EB 2.2 CRDi generally regarded as more robust than D4EA - stronger architecture, but timing belt service intervals are critical and failures are catastrophic if neglected Theta G4KC/G4KA petrol aluminium-block architecture is a welcome step forward in weight and refinement - however early units show oil consumption from piston ring wear at higher mileages Lambda G6EA V6 petrol in Santa Fe considered smooth and durable but relatively rare in UK market - replacement demand limited [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: D4EA, D4EB, G4KC, G4KA, G4GC, G6EA
years.cta: Compare 2003–2006 Hyundai Tucson and Santa Fe diesel engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: i30 & i40 Era, D4FB 1.6 CRDi Dominance & Gamma Petrol Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The i30 (2007) transformed Hyundai's UK presence and brought the D4FB 1.6 CRDi into volume production as the most widely fitted Hyundai diesel in UK history - while the Gamma 1.4/1.6 petrol family introduced a modern all-aluminium entry-level architecture. Key Changes i30 (FD) launched (2007) with D4FB 1.6 CRDi diesel and G4FA/G4FC Gamma 1.4/1.6 petrol - volume sales begin 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction Gamma G4FA 1.4 and G4FC 1.6 DOHC petrol introduced - aluminium block, chain-driven, replaces Alpha/Beta in small models D4FB 1.6 CRDi becomes Hyundai's highest-volume diesel unit in UK market i20 launched (2008) with Gamma petrol and D4FB diesel Genesis Coupe launched (2009) with Theta G4KD 2.0T turbocharged petrol and Tau 3.8 V8 Euro 4 fully enforced; Euro 5 transition preparations begin Main Engines D4FB - 1.6 CRDi diesel (i30, i20, Elantra, Accent) G4FA - 1.4 Gamma DOHC petrol (i20, i30, Accent) G4FC - 1.6 Gamma DOHC petrol (i30, Elantra, Accent) G4KD - 2.0 Theta II turbocharged petrol (Genesis Coupe, i40) D4EA - 2.0 CRDi continued (Tucson, Santa Fe) D4EB - 2.2 CRDi continued (Santa Fe) Popular Models Hyundai i30 (FD) Hyundai i20 Hyundai Santa Fe (second generation) Hyundai Tucson (first generation) Hyundai Genesis Coupe Known For D4FB 1.6 CRDi is the definitive Hyundai UK diesel of the 2007–2015 era - fuel-efficient, torquey, and widely available; however timing chain wear between 80,000–120,000 km and EGR valve clogging are cited as the two most common UK specialist issues Gamma G4FC/G4FA petrol units are reliable entry-level engines - oil consumption from valve stem seal wear beyond 80,000 miles is the primary long-term concern D4FA/D4FB diesel EGR valve blockage in stop-start urban driving is an accelerated wear pattern widely discussed in UK Hyundai owner communities - regular motorway running recommended to extend EGR life Genesis Coupe Theta G4KD turbocharged unit delivers genuinely sporty performance - rod bearing wear at high rpm under sustained hard use is an enthusiast-documented concern, foreshadowing the wider Theta II issues [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: D4FB, D4FA, G4FA, G4FC, G4KD, D4EA, D4EB, G6EA (continued)
years.cta: Get 2007–2010 Hyundai i30 and i20 engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Theta II Controversy, Nu Engine Launch & Euro 5 Transition 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The Nu four-cylinder petrol family arrived as a modern replacement for Beta in medium models, but this period is defined globally by the Theta II crankshaft debris manufacturing defect - the largest and most consequential engine recall in Hyundai's history. Key Changes Theta II G4KH/G4KJ 2.0T and G4KE/G4KG 2.4 MPI engines under recall investigation - metal debris in crankshaft oil passages causes connecting rod bearing failure and engine seizure Nu G4NA 2.0 and G4NB 1.8 introduced (2011) - replaces Beta in Elantra, i40; aluminium block, DOHC, Dual CVVT i40 saloon and estate launched (2011) with Nu 2.0 petrol, 1.7 CRDi (D4FD) diesel D4FD 1.7 CRDi introduced (2011) - new diesel for i40, ix35 ix35 launched (2010) with D4FB 1.6 CRDi and D4HA 2.0 CRDi - replaces Tucson Euro 5 compliance in force from 2011 Main Engines G4NA - 2.0 Nu DOHC petrol (Elantra MD, i40) G4NB - 1.8 Nu DOHC petrol (Elantra, i40) G4KH - 2.0 Theta II turbocharged petrol GDi (ix35 Turbo, Sonata) D4FD - 1.7 CRDi diesel (i40, ix35) D4HA - 2.0 CRDi diesel (ix35, Santa Fe, Sonata diesel) D4FB - 1.6 CRDi (i30, i20, Accent continued) Popular Models Hyundai i40 Hyundai ix35 Hyundai i30 (second generation) Hyundai Elantra (MD) Hyundai Santa Fe (third generation) Known For Theta II G4KH/G4KJ crankshaft debris defect is the most significant Hyundai engine engineering failure in the brand's history - machining swarf left in crankshaft oil drillings starves rod bearings of lubrication, leading to seizure and potential engine fire; recall covered 2011–2014 production Nu G4NA 2.0 and G4NB 1.8 are significant improvements in refinement and economy over Beta - but early timing chain wear from 70,000 miles and oil consumption concerns are reported by UK and US owner forums D4FD 1.7 CRDi in i40 delivers strong real-world economy but DPF regeneration issues in urban use and EGR valve clogging are the principal UK specialist complaints D4HA 2.0 CRDi in ix35 is generally considered the most robust diesel in the range during this period - well regarded by specialist rebuilders for long-term durability [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: G4NA, G4NB, G4KH, G4KJ, G4KE, D4FD, D4HA, D4FB, D4FC
years.cta: Get 2011–2013 Hyundai ix35, i40, and i30 replacement engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Tucson NX1 & Santa Fe Third Gen, D4HA Euro 6 Prep & New i20 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The third-generation Tucson (NX1, 2015) and refreshed Santa Fe moved to updated Euro 5/6 CRDi units, the new Gamma T-GDi turbocharged petrol appeared in the i20 and i30, and continued Theta II recall fallout reshaped Hyundai's UK reputation. Key Changes Tucson third generation (NX1) launched (2015) with D4HA 2.0 CRDi and G4KH/G4NA petrol options - UK's most popular Hyundai SUV Gamma G4FJ 1.6 T-GDi turbocharged petrol introduced (2015) - 177 hp; items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if your chassis and gearbox remain in solid condition. A rebuilt unit fitted with upgraded bearings or revised timing components typically outlasts the factory original, making it far more economical than purchasing a newer car. items.question: What are the most common Hyundai engine failures?
items.answer: The most frequent catastrophic issues include connecting rod bearing failure on 2.0/2.4L Theta II petrol engines, timing chain stretch in 1.4–1.6L Gamma units, and severe oil dilution from blocked DPF/EGR systems on 1.6L CRDi diesels. Turbocharger oil-coking also affects early Kappa T-GDi engines driven primarily on short urban trips. Because these Hyundai engine failure symptoms rarely allow for safe preventative repair, full replacement is usually the most reliable long-term solution. → Compare rebuilt Hyundai engine prices before minor damage becomes catastrophic
